questions.text: I tend not to notice when a person's energy changes after reading a message on their phone.
I find that my nervous system seems to have a natural governor that prevents emotional spikes.
I routinely visualize my own future success and make decisions to bring that vision closer.
I feel that predictable routines are the foundation on which creative breakthroughs are built.
I find that my emotions are not background music — they are the main event.
I feel that leading well means deciding on behalf of those who cannot yet see the full picture.
I hold the view that the moral concern I feel for those who are suffering is the experience that most deeply and authentically expresses who I am as a moral agent.
I find it satisfying to override inertia and get things moving.
I notice the difference between a genuine invitation and a courtesy one.
I rarely sense when a person is anchoring a negotiation through seemingly casual conversation.
I believe I have a talent for seeing what needs to be done and making it happen.
I find that others naturally see me as a reliable collaborator.
My emotional responses are so vivid that I sometimes wonder if other people are experiencing a different reality.
I tend to put my career milestones at the center of my life decisions.
I notice that I can be in the presence of someone who is deeply distressed without my own system mirroring their state.
I believe that deliberate emotional composure during crises is what turns concern into effective action.
I enjoy being assigned a role and doing it well.
I maintain a consistent filing system that anyone else could navigate.
I notice that my pulse and breathing remain steady even during emotionally charged discussions.
I tend to be the last person to realize when a social event is winding down.
I believe that when I maintain objectivity during emotionally demanding situations, I am honoring the complexity of the problems I face.
I let the scope of a task reveal itself as I work rather than defining it upfront.
I naturally position myself as a connector at the periphery, linking others together.
I practice new skills in a structured, progressive way, building from simple to complex.
I feel a deep sense of duty to use everything I have in service of others.
I start projects with what I have and acquire tools as the need becomes clear.
I believe that emotional composure during crisis situations is a form of strength that is most apparent in the quality of the decisions it enables.
I believe that moral maturity includes the willingness to let the weight of others' suffering shape my understanding of what matters.
I believe that the moral weight of suffering is the truth that should humble every attempt to reduce ethics to a matter of rules or calculations.
I notice that the emotional charge of a holiday or celebration stays with me long after the event is over.
I handle errands and tasks as they come up, even if that means extra trips.
I hold the view that a person who takes care of themselves is in the best position to live a good life.
I notice when someone includes a disclaimer before a statement because they are anticipating pushback from a specific person.
I often intuit the backstory between people I have just met based on how they interact.
I find lasting satisfaction in contributions that no one knows I made.
I routinely turn down personal opportunities if they would take me away from people who rely on me.
